scenario: A tenant requests an emotional support dog but my lease says no pets
correct_action: You must allow the ESA as a reasonable accommodation under the Fair Housing Act. Request a letter from a healthcare professional verifying the disability-related need. You cannot charge pet rent or pet deposit. You CAN require the tenant to clean up after the animal and can deny if the animal poses a direct threat or would cause substantial damage.
common_mistake: Denying the request or charging pet fees — this is FHA discrimination
penalty_if_wrong: Up to $16,000 first violation, $65,000 subsequent violations, plus tenant lawsuit damages

scenario: Tenant hasn't paid rent for 15 days, I want to change the locks
correct_action: Do NOT change the locks. This is an illegal self-help eviction. Serve the proper notice to pay or quit (check your state's required notice period — typically 3-14 days). If tenant doesn't pay within the notice period, file an unlawful detainer in court. Only a sheriff/marshal can physically remove a tenant after a court order.
common_mistake: Self-help eviction (changing locks, shutting off utilities, removing belongings) — illegal in all 50 states
penalty_if_wrong: Tenant can sue for actual damages, punitive damages, and attorney fees. Some states impose penalties of 2-3x monthly rent.

scenario: I'm renting a unit built in 1965 and didn't disclose lead paint
correct_action: Immediately provide the EPA disclosure form and pamphlet to current tenants. Going forward, always provide lead paint disclosure before lease signing for any property built before 1978. Keep records of disclosure for 3 years minimum.
common_mistake: Not providing lead paint disclosure for pre-1978 properties
penalty_if_wrong: Up to $16,000 per violation per day. Federal EPA enforcement.

scenario: Tenant is asking for a wheelchair ramp to the front entrance
correct_action: Under the Fair Housing Act, this is a reasonable modification request for a disability. The TENANT is generally responsible for paying for modifications inside the unit. For common area modifications (like a ramp to the entrance), the landlord must allow it and the tenant pays. You can require the tenant to restore the modification at move-out in some cases. You cannot deny without an individualized assessment of whether it's an undue financial/administrative burden.
common_mistake: Denying disability accommodation requests without individualized assessment
penalty_if_wrong: FHA discrimination penalty up to $16,000-$65,000 plus ADA-related damages

scenario: I want to raise rent by 20% for a month-to-month tenant
correct_action: Check if your state/city has rent control (if yes, increases may be capped). If no rent control: provide proper written notice (typically 30 days for increases under 10%, 60-90 days for increases over 10% in many states like California). The increase must take effect at the beginning of a rental period. Make sure the notice is delivered properly (certified mail, hand delivery, or as specified in lease).
common_mistake: Not providing adequate notice or exceeding rent control caps in regulated areas
penalty_if_wrong: In rent-controlled areas: fines, rent rollback, tenant lawsuit. In non-regulated areas: notice may be void, requiring you to start over.

scenario: Tenant moved out and left the unit damaged, I want to keep the entire deposit
correct_action: You can only deduct for damage BEYOND normal wear and tear. Document all damage with photos. Get repair estimates or receipts. Provide an itemized list of deductions within your state's deadline (14-60 days depending on state). Return the remaining deposit. Common mistake: charging for painting after 2+ years (normal wear) or carpet replacement without proof of damage beyond wear.
common_mistake: Keeping entire deposit without itemization, missing the return deadline, charging for normal wear and tear
penalty_if_wrong: Many states impose 2-3x penalty: if deposit is $2000 and you wrongfully keep it, you may owe $4000-$6000 plus attorney fees.

scenario: I want to show the unit to prospective tenants while current tenant still lives there
correct_action: Provide proper notice before each showing (typically 24 hours). Check your state's specific requirement — some require 24 hours, others 48. The lease may specify the notice period. You cannot enter without notice except in emergencies. You must show during reasonable hours (typically 9am-8pm). The tenant has the right to be present during the showing but cannot unreasonably refuse entry with proper notice.
common_mistake: Entering without notice, showing outside reasonable hours, or entering without a legitimate purpose
penalty_if_wrong: Tenant can sue for violation of quiet enjoyment, break the lease, or file for injunctive relief.

scenario: Tenant is running a business out of the rental unit
correct_action: Check your lease — most prohibit commercial activity. If the lease prohibits it, serve a notice to cure (violation notice). If the tenant doesn't stop, you can proceed with eviction for lease violation. However, be careful: some states protect home-based businesses that don't create nuisance or increased traffic. A tenant working from home on a laptop is generally acceptable; a tenant running a daycare or manufacturing is different. Consider whether the business creates nuisance, noise, or additional liability before acting.
common_mistake: Evicting without checking whether the state protects home-based businesses, or not having a lease clause prohibiting commercial use
penalty_if_wrong: Wrongful eviction lawsuit, especially if the activity is protected or the lease doesn't clearly prohibit it.

scenario: Tenant is hoarding and the unit is becoming a health hazard
correct_action: Document the conditions with photos. If there's an immediate safety hazard (fire risk, blocked exits, pest infestation), issue a notice to cure. If the tenant requests accommodation for a disability (hoarding can be a disability under FHA), engage in the interactive process. Give reasonable time to clean up with a specific deadline. If the tenant fails to comply after reasonable notice and accommodation, you may proceed with eviction for lease violation (habitability).
common_mistake: Immediately evicting without considering FHA reasonable accommodation requirements for hoarding as a disability
penalty_if_wrong: FHA discrimination claim: up to $16,000-$65,000 in civil penalties plus actual damages and attorney fees

scenario: Tenant is subletting on Airbnb without permission
correct_action: Check the lease for subletting prohibition. If prohibited: 1) Document evidence (screenshots of listing, guest reviews). 2) Issue a notice to cure requiring the tenant to remove the listing and stop short-term rentals. 3) If they don't comply within the notice period, file for eviction for lease violation. 4) If the lease doesn't prohibit subletting, check state law — many states require landlord consent. 5) Consider adding a no-short-term-rental clause to future leases.
common_mistake: Shutting off utilities or changing locks to force the tenant to stop — this is an illegal self-help eviction
penalty_if_wrong: Tenant can sue for actual damages, punitive damages (2-3x monthly rent in some states), and attorney fees

scenario: Applicant has a criminal record from 10 years ago
correct_action: Do NOT automatically reject. Per HUD guidance, blanket criminal history bans may violate the Fair Housing Act through disparate impact. Instead: 1) Consider the nature and severity of the offense (violent crimes vs. minor offenses). 2) Consider how long ago it occurred (10 years is a long time). 3) Consider whether the offense is relevant to being a tenant (e.g., prior eviction or property damage vs. unrelated crime). 4) Apply the same individualized assessment to all applicants. 5) Document your reasoning for any rejection. 6) Check if your state/city bans criminal history screening.
common_mistake: Automatically rejecting all applicants with any criminal record — this is a Fair Housing Act violation
penalty_if_wrong: HUD investigation, FHA discrimination penalty up to $16,000-$65,000, plus lawsuit damages

scenario: The rental unit has no carbon monoxide detectors and a tenant gets sick
correct_action: Install carbon monoxide detectors immediately. In 27+ states, CO detectors are legally required in rental units with fuel-burning appliances or attached garages. Even in states without specific requirements, you have a duty of care. If a tenant gets sick from CO poisoning: 1) Ensure medical attention. 2) Have the unit inspected for CO sources. 3) Repair any gas leaks or ventilation issues. 4) Install CO detectors on every floor near sleeping areas. 5) Document the installation. 6) Your landlord insurance may cover liability, but failing to install detectors when required by law can negate coverage.
common_mistake: Not installing CO detectors because the state doesn't specifically require them — you still have a duty of care
penalty_if_wrong: Liability for medical bills, potential wrongful death lawsuit, insurance may deny coverage for negligence

scenario: Tenant filed a complaint with the housing inspector and I want to evict them
correct_action: Do NOT evict in retaliation. Almost all 50 states have anti-retaliation laws protecting tenants who file complaints. If you evict within 6-12 months of a complaint, it's presumed retaliatory and you'll have to prove it wasn't. Instead: 1) Address the issues the inspector identified. 2) If you have separate, documented lease violations that predate the complaint, you can pursue those — but document the timeline carefully. 3) Wait until well after the complaint period to take action on borderline issues. 4. Consult a landlord-tenant attorney before issuing any notice to a tenant who recently filed a complaint.
common_mistake: Issuing an eviction notice shortly after a tenant files a housing complaint — this creates a presumption of retaliation
penalty_if_wrong: Retaliation claim: tenant can recover actual damages, punitive damages, attorney fees, and the eviction will be dismissed

scenario: Tenant abandoned the unit with 3 months left on the lease and left furniture behind
correct_action: 1) Determine if this is a true abandonment (tenant clearly moved out, stopped paying, no personal items of value). Some states require specific abandonment notices. 2) Document the condition with photos and witnesses. 3) Store the abandoned property per your state's abandoned property law (typically 15-30 days with written notice to the tenant's last known address). 4) Make reasonable efforts to re-rent the unit (mitigate damages). 5) The tenant remains liable for rent until the unit is re-rented or the lease expires. 6) Apply the security deposit to unpaid rent and damages. 7) If deposit doesn't cover it, sue the tenant in small claims court for the balance. 8) Do not dispose of the tenant's property until the state-mandated storage period expires.
common_mistake: Immediately throwing away the tenant's property without following the state's abandoned property law
penalty_if_wrong: Liability for the value of disposed property plus potential punitive damages (varies by state, up to several times the value)

scenario: Tenant's guest caused damage to the property
correct_action: The tenant is responsible for damage caused by their guests, just as they are for their own damage. Document the damage with photos, get repair estimates, and deduct from the security deposit per your state's itemization requirements. If the damage exceeds the deposit, you can sue the tenant in small claims court. The tenant cannot avoid liability by claiming 'my guest did it, not me' — the lease makes the tenant responsible for all occupants and guests.
common_mistake: Trying to go after the guest directly instead of the tenant on the lease
penalty_if_wrong: Wasted time and legal fees pursuing the wrong party; the lease holder is responsible

scenario: Tenant wants to paint the walls a different color
correct_action: You can approve or deny this request — it's your property. If you approve: 1) Require the tenant to use a professional painter or approve specific paint colors. 2) Specify in writing that the tenant must restore the original color at move-out (or you can deduct from deposit). 3) Require a damage deposit for the repainting. If you deny: The lease likely prohibits alterations without landlord consent. Issue a notice to cure if they paint without permission.
common_mistake: Allowing painting verbally without written terms about restoration
penalty_if_wrong: Tenant paints an undesirable color and you have no written agreement requiring restoration

scenario: Tenant is running a daycare business from the rental
correct_action: Check your lease for business use prohibition. Running a daycare creates liability (injuries to children), may violate zoning, increases wear and traffic, and may void your insurance. If the lease prohibits business use: 1) Issue a notice to cure/vacate. 2) If they don't stop, file for eviction. If the lease is silent: 1) You may still prohibit it if it creates a nuisance or increased liability. 2) Check local zoning ordinances. 3) Your landlord insurance likely excludes commercial daycare coverage — you could be personally liable for injuries.
common_mistake: Ignoring the daycare because the tenant 'seems responsible' — one child injury could cost millions
penalty_if_wrong: Liability for child injuries on your property, insurance denial for commercial use, zoning violations

scenario: Tenant died and family wants to break the lease
correct_action: Most states require the estate to continue paying rent until the lease expires or the unit is re-rented. Process: 1) Request a death certificate and executor/administrator documentation. 2) The executor is responsible for the lease obligations. 3) You must make reasonable efforts to re-rent the unit (mitigate damages). 4) The security deposit goes to the estate after proper itemization. 5) Some states have special provisions for military death or domestic violence situations. 6) Do not enter the unit or remove belongings until you have legal authority — the tenant's property passes to the estate.
common_mistake: Immediately re-entering the unit and disposing of the deceased tenant's belongings
penalty_if_wrong: Liability to the estate for wrongfully disposed property, potential wrongful eviction claim

scenario: A service animal bit another tenant
correct_action: Under the Fair Housing Act, you cannot ban the service animal, but you CAN take action if the animal poses a direct threat. 1) Document the incident with photos, witness statements, and medical reports. 2) Issue a notice to the handler requiring them to control the animal. 3) If the animal continues to pose a threat, you can require removal of the specific animal (not a blanket ban on all assistance animals). 4) You must conduct an individualized assessment — one bite doesn't automatically justify removal if the handler takes corrective action. 5. Consult a fair housing attorney before removing any assistance animal.
common_mistake: Immediately banning all assistance animals after one incident — this violates the FHA
penalty_if_wrong: FHA discrimination claim: $16,000-$65,000 in penalties plus the bitten tenant's injury claim

scenario: Tenant installed a security camera pointing at shared areas
correct_action: Tenants generally can install security cameras INSIDE their unit and at their front door. However: 1) Cameras cannot record shared/common areas (hallways, parking lots, other tenants' doors) in many states — this may violate other tenants' privacy rights. 2) Check your lease for alteration/Installation rules. 3) You can require written consent for exterior modifications. 4) If the camera records audio, some states have two-party consent recording laws. 5) You can require the tenant to reposition or remove cameras that record neighbors. 6) Document the issue and issue a notice to cure if the lease prohibits it.
common_mistake: Ignoring the camera because 'it's their security' — you could be liable if it records other tenants
penalty_if_wrong: Privacy lawsuit from other tenants, potential landlord liability for allowing surveillance

scenario: California tenant is being evicted — what are the special rules?
correct_action: California has strict tenant protections: 1) Just Cause Eviction: Under AB 1482, landlords must have a valid reason (fault or no-fault) to evict tenants who have lived there 12+ months. 2) Relocation assistance: For no-fault evictions, you must pay one month's rent OR waive the last month's rent. 3) Rent cap: Annual increases limited to 5% + CPI (max 10%) under AB 1482. 4) 60-day notice required for tenancy over 1 year. 5) 30-day notice for under 1 year. 6) Some cities (LA, SF, Oakland) have stricter rent control. 7. Ellis Act: For removing units from rental market, specific notice and relocation payments required.
common_mistake: Issuing a 30-day no-cause eviction in a Just Cause jurisdiction — this is illegal under AB 1482
penalty_if_wrong: Eviction dismissed, tenant awarded damages, potential $1,000-$5,000 per violation penalty

scenario: New York tenant hasn't paid rent — eviction process
correct_action: New York eviction process: 1) Serve a 14-day written demand for rent (Rent Demand). 2) If unpaid, file a Non-Payment Petition in housing court. 3. The court will schedule a hearing (can take weeks in NYC). 4) If you win, a Marshal/Sheriff executes a Warrant of Eviction. 5) In NYC, tenants have the right to legal counsel (Right to Counsel law). 6) Rent stabilization/rent control tenants have additional protections. 7) The Housing Stability and Tenant Protection Act of 2019 limits late fees to $50 or 5% of rent (whichever is less). 8) Security deposits limited to 1 month's rent.
common_mistake: Filing eviction without the required 14-day rent demand notice
penalty_if_wrong: Eviction case dismissed, have to start over (adds weeks to the process)

scenario: Texas landlord wants to enter without notice for inspection
correct_action: Texas law does not specify a notice period for landlord entry (unlike most states). However: 1) The lease controls — if the lease requires 24-48 hours notice, you must follow it. 2) Texas law requires 'reasonable notice' even without a specific statute. 3) Emergency entry (fire, flood, gas) is always allowed without notice. 4) Texas allows self-help eviction ONLY if the lease specifically includes a clause allowing it AND you give the tenant reasonable notice to vacate (this is rare and risky). 5) Best practice: Always give at least 24 hours written notice, even though Texas doesn't mandate it.
common_mistake: Entering without notice because 'Texas doesn't require it' — your lease probably does
penalty_if_wrong: Tenant can sue for breach of quiet enjoyment, break the lease, or sue for invasion of privacy

scenario: Florida landlord dealing with hurricane damage to rental
correct_action: Florida has specific hurricane/natural disaster provisions: 1) If the unit is uninhabitable, the tenant may terminate the lease with 7 days written notice (Florida Statute 83.215). 2) You must return the security deposit within 15 days if no damages. 3) If the unit is damaged but habitable, you must repair within a reasonable time. 4) Rent abatement: The tenant may reduce rent proportionally for the period the unit is partially unusable. 5) Florida landlords must maintain windstorm/hurricane insurance if the lease requires it. 6) Document all damage with photos immediately. 7. Check FEMA assistance eligibility for both landlord and tenant.
common_mistake: Demanding full rent while the unit has hurricane damage making it partially uninhabitable
penalty_if_wrong: Tenant can legally withhold/abate rent, terminate lease, or sue for breach of habitability

scenario: Oregon tenant — rent increase rules under statewide rent control
correct_action: Oregon has statewide rent control (SB 608): 1) Annual rent increases capped at 7% + CPI (typically around 9-10% total). 2) No-cause evictions banned after 12 months of tenancy. 3) You must give 90 days notice for rent increases (not 30). 4) For tenancy under 1 year: 60 days notice for no-cause. 5. Relocation assistance required for no-cause evictions (equal to 1 month's rent, paid to tenant). 6. Portland has additional tenant protections beyond state law. 7. The cap applies to all residential rentals except those built in the last 15 years.
common_mistake: Issuing a 30-day rent increase notice — Oregon requires 90 days
penalty_if_wrong: Rent increase voided, must re-issue with 90-day notice (delays increase by 2 months)

scenario: Puerto Rico landlord — Spanish language requirement for legal documents
correct_action: Puerto Rico has unique requirements: 1) Lease agreements and legal notices should be in Spanish (the official language). English versions can be provided but Spanish is legally primary. 2) The 2020 Civil Code reform updated landlord-tenant law. 3) Security deposits: 2 months (unfurnished), 3 months (furnished). 4) Return within 30 days with itemized deductions. 5) Eviction requires court order (no self-help). 6) Some municipalities have rent stabilization (especially San Juan metro). 7. Court proceedings are in Spanish. 8. Consider bilingual leases (Spanish primary, English secondary). 9. The Puerto Rico Public Housing Administration (AVP) oversees housing programs.
common_mistake: Using English-only lease in Puerto Rico — courts may not enforce it properly
penalty_if_wrong: Lease provisions may be unenforceable in Puerto Rico courts, delays in eviction proceedings

scenario: Washington state tenant requesting reasonable accommodation for marijuana use
correct_action: Washington state legalized recreational marijuana, creating a unique situation: 1) You CAN prohibit smoking marijuana in the lease (smoke-free housing rules apply to all smoking). 2) You CANNOT prohibit marijuana edibles/topicals if they don't create a nuisance (similar to other legal substances). 3) If the tenant has a medical marijuana card and requests accommodation, engage in the interactive process under FHA/Washington Law Against Discrimination. 4. Federal law still classifies marijuana as illegal — HUD properties must prohibit it. 5. Washington's Landlord-Tenant Act applies. 6. You can restrict cultivation/growing plants per the lease.
common_mistake: Banning all marijuana use when the tenant has a medical card and uses edibles — may violate disability accommodation
penalty_if_wrong: Fair housing/Washington discrimination claim, potential penalties and damages
